prompt: Which summary best fits 2 Chronicles 1?
choices_json: Solomon offers sacrifice at Gibeon and asks God for wisdom rather than riches or long life. God grants him wisdom, knowledge, wealth, and honor beyond any king before or after him. The chapter establishes that Solomon's greatness begins with dependence on God, not self-sufficiency.

Solomon arranges for cedar, skilled labor, and abundant materials so the temple can be built with excellence. He writes to Hiram and explains that the house will be for the great God whose name fills heaven and earth. The chapter shows deliberate preparation before construction begins.

Solomon begins construction of the temple on Mount Moriah, the place associated with Abraham's sacrifice. The chapter carefully describes the dimensions, materials, and ornamentation of the house. The emphasis falls on holiness, continuity, and the visible beauty of worship.

The chapter details the bronze and gold furnishings that equip the temple for worship. Hiram's craftsmanship produces the great sea, the pillars, the stands, and the utensils used in service. The list underscores that sacred space is furnished carefully for holy use.
correct_answer: Solomon offers sacrifice at Gibeon and asks God for wisdom rather than riches or long life. God grants him wisdom, knowledge, wealth, and honor beyond any king before or after him. The chapter establishes that Solomon's greatness begins with dependence on God, not self-sufficiency.
explanation: Solomon offers sacrifice at Gibeon and asks God for wisdom rather than riches or long life. God grants him wisdom, knowledge, wealth, and honor beyond any king before or after him. The chapter establishes that Solomon's greatness begins with dependence on God, not self-sufficiency. (2 Chronicles 1:7)

prompt: Which summary best fits 2 Chronicles 16?
choices_json: Solomon begins construction of the temple on Mount Moriah, the place associated with Abraham's sacrifice. The chapter carefully describes the dimensions, materials, and ornamentation of the house. The emphasis falls on holiness, continuity, and the visible beauty of worship.

When Baasha threatens Judah, Asa turns to Aram for help instead of relying on the LORD. Hanani rebukes the king for depending on human alliances, and Asa reacts with anger rather than repentance. The chapter marks the shift from early faithfulness to late compromise.

Solomon offers sacrifice at Gibeon and asks God for wisdom rather than riches or long life. God grants him wisdom, knowledge, wealth, and honor beyond any king before or after him. The chapter establishes that Solomon's greatness begins with dependence on God, not self-sufficiency.

Solomon arranges for cedar, skilled labor, and abundant materials so the temple can be built with excellence. He writes to Hiram and explains that the house will be for the great God whose name fills heaven and earth. The chapter shows deliberate preparation before construction begins.
correct_answer: When Baasha threatens Judah, Asa turns to Aram for help instead of relying on the LORD. Hanani rebukes the king for depending on human alliances, and Asa reacts with anger rather than repentance. The chapter marks the shift from early faithfulness to late compromise.
explanation: When Baasha threatens Judah, Asa turns to Aram for help instead of relying on the LORD. Hanani rebukes the king for depending on human alliances, and Asa reacts with anger rather than repentance. The chapter marks the shift from early faithfulness to late compromise. (2 Chronicles 16:2)

Solomon begins construction of the temple on Mount Moriah, the place associated with Abraham's sacrifice. The chapter carefully describes the dimensions, materials, and ornamentation of the house. The emphasis falls on holiness, continuity, and the visible beauty of worship.

Uzziah prospers while he seeks the LORD, but pride leads him to intrude into priestly duties. When he burns incense in the temple, leprosy breaks out and he is removed from public life. The chapter contrasts blessing under humility with judgment under self-exaltation.

The chapter contrasts blessing under humility with judgment under self-exaltation. (2 Chronicles 26:5)
